perturbation.original_text: Each party represents and warrants to the other that: (i) it has the right, and will continue to have the right during the Term, to grant the other party all of the rights granted to it under this Agreement, (ii) neither this Agreement nor the transactions contemplated hereby will cause a violation of any other agreement to which it is a party, and (iii) it has complied, and will comply, with all laws, rules and regulations applicable to the performance of its duties and obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party represents and warrants to the other that: (i) it has the right, and will continue to have the right during the Term, to grant the other party all of the rights granted to it under this Agreement, (ii) neither this Agreement nor the transactions contemplated hereby will cause a violation of any other agreement to which it is a party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and (iii) it has complied, and will comply, with all laws, rules and regulations applicable to the performance of its duties and obligations under this Agreement', we are creating a situation where a company doesn't explicitly agree to comply with all laws, rules, and regulations. By removing this agreement, this could be interpreted as a violation of general legal compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts general legal compliance as it pertains to fair business practices. Section 5(a) of the Federal Trade Commission Act [15 U.S. Code § 45(a)] declares that 'Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are hereby declared unlawful.' By removing the clause that states the party will comply with laws and regulations, the agreement implicitly opens the door to potential unfair practices, thereby conflicting with the FTC Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce , and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are hereby declared unlawful. (2) The Commission is hereby empowered and directed to prevent persons, partnerships, or corporations , except banks, savings and loan institutions described in section 57a(f)(3) of this title , Federal credit unions described in section 57a(f)(4) of this title , common carriers subject to the Acts to regulate commerce, air carriers and foreign air carriers subject to part A of subtitle VII of title 49, and persons, partnerships, or corporations insofar as they are subject to the Packers and Stockyards Act, 1921 , as amended [ 7 U.S.C. 181 et seq.], except as provided in section 406(b) of said Act [ 7 U.S.C. 227(b) ], from using unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce. (3) This subsection shall not apply to unfair methods of competition involving commerce with foreign nations (other than import commerce ) unless— (A) such methods of competition have a direct, substantial, and reasonably foreseeable effect— (i) on commerce which is not commerce with foreign nations, or on import commerce with foreign nations; or (ii) on export commerce with foreign nations, of a person engaged in such commerce in the United States; and (B) such effect gives rise to a claim under the provisions of this subsection, other than this paragraph. If this subsection applies to such methods of compet
perturbation.justification: The removal of the clause ensuring compliance with all laws, rules, and regulations creates a contradiction. 15 U.S. Code § 45(a)(1), as shown in both scraped snippets, declares unlawful 'Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.' By omitting the explicit agreement to comply with laws, the contract now implicitly permits actions that could be deemed 'unfair or deceptive,' directly conflicting with the FTC Act.

perturbation.original_text: 8.5 Costs, Attorney's Fee on Breach. If any action is brought by either party under this Agreement whether by suit, arbitration or otherwise by reason of any claim or cause of action against the other, arising out of or in connection with any breach or other non-performance of the provision of this Agreement, then the party which is successful upon any final determination of such claim or cause shall be entitled to recovery of its actual reasonable costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred therein.
perturbation.changed_text:
8.1 Complete Agreement. This Agreement and any attachments, exhibits, or schedules attached to hereto contains the complete agreement between the parties and supersede any prior understandings, representations, covenants or agreements between the parties, written or oral, with respect to said subject matter.

8.5 Costs, Attorney's Fee on Breach. If any action is brought by either party under this Agreement whether by suit, arbitration or otherwise by reason of any claim or cause of action against the other, arising out of or in connection with any breach or other non-performance of the provision of this Agreement, then the party which is successful upon any final determination of such claim or cause shall be entitled to recovery of its actual reasonable costs incurred therein.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "reasonable attorney's fees" we remove the successful party to recover compensation, undermining the principle that a party should be made whole after prevailing in legal action. While attorney's fees are not always recoverable, this provision initially allows it.
perturbation.law_explanation: In California, Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 generally allows parties to contract for the payment of attorney's fees. By removing 'reasonable attorney's fees', the modification contradicts the contractual intention to allow the successful party to recover such fees, potentially leaving the successful party with uncovered legal costs.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the provision for recovery of 'reasonable attorney's fees', which, while not mandated by statute, is permitted by contract according to California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021. The original text explicitly allowed for the recovery of attorney's fees, and removing this contradicts the initial contractual intention, as a contract can provide for attorney's fees. CCP 1021 states that '...the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties...'. By removing the attorneys fees, the agreement is altered, potentially leaving the prevailing party without full compensation and contradicting the principle of making the party whole.

perturbation.original_text: 8.9 Notices. Any notice, request, instruction or other documents permitted or required to be given hereunder by any party to the other parties shall be in writing and delivered personally, by certified U.S. Mail return receipt requested, by nationally recognized reputable overnight courier, or by facsimile transmission as follows: If to XLI: Bosch International 3753 Howard Hughes Parkway, Suite 200 Las Vegas, NV 89169 Attn: James Schramm Phone : (310) 871-4046 If to WCC: RMF Empire Inc. 2101 W. Empire Ave. Burbank, CA 91504 Attn: Ryan Friedlinghaus Phone: (818) 237-1287 A party receiving a notice delivered personally shall sign a receipt therefore. Notices by U.S. Mail or facsimile transmission shall contain an acknowledgement of receipt. A party receiving a notice by facsimile or Email shall acknowledge receipt by return facsimile or reply email within two (2) business days of receipt. A party receiving notice by U.S. Mail shall place the acknowledgment in the U.S. Mail, postage prepaid, within two (2) business days of receipt.
perturbation.changed_text:
8.4 Section Headings. The various section headings are for convenience only and shall not affect the meaning or interpretation of this Agreement.

8.9 Notices. Any notice, request, instruction or other documents permitted or required to be given hereunder by any party to the other parties shall be in writing and delivered personally, by certified U.S. Mail return receipt requested, by nationally recognized reputable overnight courier, or by facsimile transmission as follows: If to XLI: Bosch International 3753 Howard Hughes Parkway, Suite 200 Las Vegas, NV 89169 Attn: James Schramm Phone : (310) 871-4046 If to WCC: RMF Empire Inc. 2101 W. Empire Ave. Burbank, CA 91504 Attn: Ryan Friedlinghaus Phone: (818) 237-1287 A party receiving a notice delivered personally shall sign a receipt therefore. Notices by U.S. Mail or facsimile transmission shall contain an acknowledgement of receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to acknowledge receipt of notices sent by facsimile/email or US Mail undermines the legal certainty of the agreement. Proof of notice is essential for enforcing obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1565 requires the consent of the parties to a contract to be free, mutual, and communicated by each to the other. Removing the acknowledgment of receipt provisions creates ambiguity as to whether effective communication occurred, potentially rendering the consent not properly communicated and thus impacting the validity of the contract formation.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Consent [1565 - 1590] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1565. The consent of the parties to a contract must be: 1. Free; 2. Mutual; and, 3. Communicated by each to the other. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for acknowledging receipt of notices sent by facsimile/email or US Mail, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. This weakens the 'communicated by each to the other' requirement of consent, as stated in California Civil Code § 1565, described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 also states this requirement. Without acknowledgment, proof of proper communication is uncertain, thus undermining the legal enforceability of the contract.
